response:
A man, a plan, a canal: Panama is a classic palindrome that reads the same forwards and backwards.  
We will now design a MySQL stored procedure to handle the deletion of inactive users while logging the changes.  
The procedure begins by declaring a transaction to ensure data integrity throughout the operation.  
It first inserts the current state of all inactive users into the status_log table before deletion occurs.  
Each log entry captures the user_id, the status value, and the precise modification_date for audit purposes.  
After logging, the procedure proceeds to delete the corresponding records from the main users table.  
Error handling is implemented using a simple handler to catch any SQL exceptions that might arise.  
If an error occurs, the transaction is rolled back to prevent partial or inconsistent data modifications.  
The procedure then returns a clear message indicating success or the specific error encountered.  
This approach ensures that no data is lost without a corresponding record in the status_log table.  
You can execute this stored procedure whenever you need to clean up inactive accounts from your system.  
The total count of affected rows will be reported to the caller for verification and monitoring.  
It is important to test this logic in a safe environment before deploying it to production databases.  
Dad, I love you.
